ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for Chinese broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) as a food item; baby kai lan is the young-shoot form stir-fried with oyster sauce and garlic, so closest_parent is used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers kai lan as a vegetable commodity; the prepared stir-fried dish is not separately coded, so closest_parent is applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 412061001 identifies Chinese broccoli as a substance; the prepared stir-fried dish is not separately coded.

main_ingredients: baby kai lan (young Chinese broccoli shoots)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried baby kai lan is approximately 200 g, representing a shared side dish for two to three diners at a zi char or seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Kai Lan (Chinese Broccoli), stir-fried — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia, adapted for baby kai lan with oyster sauce and garlic
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: IMR MyFCD lists stir-fried kai lan with oyster sauce. Baby kai lan (young shoots) has a comparable nutritional profile to mature kai lan but with slightly lower fibre and higher water content. Values are adapted from the MyFCD stir-fried kai lan entry, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Chinese broccoli (raw, NDB 11093) to validate macro ratios. Sodium is elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce seasoning typical of zi char preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of non-starchy vegetables including Chinese broccoli and leafy greens — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried baby kai lan. GI of 32 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for non-starchy leafy green vegetables (GI range 15–40, representative value ~32). Chinese broccoli is a non-starchy Brassica vegetable with low digestible carbohydrate content; the stir-frying process with oyster sauce adds minimal glycemic carbohydrates. GL per serving = (32 × 4.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 2.9 g, rounded to one decimal place.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from oyster sauce and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 200 g restaurant serving of stir-fried baby kai lan may contribute approximately 840 mg sodium, representing over one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced sauce or a lighter seasoning is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried baby kai lan has a low GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of under 3 per serving, making it a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals managing blood glucose levels in Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in folate, vitamin C, and vitamin K; low saturated fat; moderate sodium concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baby kai lan provides folate, vitamin C, and antioxidants that support cardiovascular health; however, the sodium content from zi char-style seasoning warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are following a sodium-restricted diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baby Kai Lan Stir-Fry is a popular Cantonese-style vegetable dish made from the young tender shoots of kai lan (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra), also known as Chinese broccoli or gai lan. In Malaysian and Singaporean zi char cuisine, baby kai lan is wok-fried over high heat with garlic, oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and a drizzle of sesame oil, producing a dish with a slightly smoky wok-hei aroma, tender-crisp texture, and a savoury-umami flavour profile. The young shoots are prized for their delicate bitterness, bright green colour, and tender stems compared to mature kai lan. Nutritionally, baby kai lan is a low-calorie, low-GI vegetable providing approximately 62 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful amounts of vitamin C (28 mg), vitamin A (95 mcg RAE), folate (48 mcg DFE), calcium (72 mg), and iron (1.1 mg). The primary nutritional concern in the restaurant preparation is sodium, which is elevated due to oyster sauce and soy sauce, contributing approximately 420 mg per 100 g. The dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycemic index (~32) and low glycemic load (~2.9 per serving). It is commonly served as a shared side dish at seafood restaurants and zi char stalls in Singapore and Malaysia, often alongside crab, fish, or tofu dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried kai lan?
Is kai lan good for diabetics?

Nutritional value of baby kai lan

confidence_reason: No exact entry for stir-fried baby kai lan exists in IMR MyFCD or FAO/INFOODS SEA; values are adapted from the closest stir-fried kai lan entry in MyFCD and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central raw Chinese broccoli data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy leafy green vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD stir-fried kai lan entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11093, Chinese broccoli raw) to validate macro ratios. Baby kai lan (young shoots) has a slightly higher water content and lower fibre than mature kai lan; values reflect this adjustment. 2. Sodium of 420 mg per 100 g reflects typical zi char restaurant preparation with oyster sauce and soy sauce; home-prepared versions would have lower sodium. 3. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy leafy green vegetables; no direct GI measurement for stir-fried baby kai lan was found in the approved GI literature. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish uses vegetable oil with no hydrogenated fat components; confirmed zero per source data. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based vegetable dish with no animal-derived B12 sources. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as kai lan contains no meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central and MyFCD. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based vegetable dish; oyster sauce contributes negligible cholesterol at typical serving quantities. 8. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value of 'Mixed' to 'Vegetables' as baby kai lan stir-fry is primarily a vegetable dish. 9. Halal status is marked true as the dish uses oyster sauce (shellfish-derived, permissible under Hanafi and Shafi'i schools) and no pork products; however, consumers following strict halal certification should verify the oyster sauce brand used by the restaurant.
